headline: Anthropic Mythos 1 surfaces in Claude Code and Claude Security ahead of broader release
summary: Anthropic is quietly moving Mythos 1 (tagged claude-mythos-1-preview) toward broader availability. The model appeared briefly inside Claude Code and Claude Security before vanishing, and is already active in vulnerability discovery programs on AWS and Google Cloud. Through Project Glasswing, Anthropic and partners identified 10,000+ critical/high-severity vulnerabilities. Claude Opus 4.8 is separately rumored to be in development.
foryou: Mythos 1 coming to Claude Code means you will soon have a model tuned for security-aware agentic workflows — worth watching its exploit-detection primitives for integration into your pipelines.

headline: DeepSeek makes 75% V4 Pro price cut permanent, widening gap against Western frontier models
summary: DeepSeek permanently cut V4 Pro prices by 75%, a promotion originally set to expire at month end. Pricing now sits well below OpenAI GPT-5, Anthropic Claude Opus 4.7, and Google Gemini 3.5 Flash. The gap is widest against frontier reasoning models that enterprise customers use for demanding workloads, effectively locking in a new floor for the AI price war.
foryou: If you run inference-heavy agentic pipelines, DeepSeek V4 Pro is now a permanent cost-floor reference point worth benchmarking against your current stack.

headline: Gemini 3.5 Flash (Low): 45% fewer output tokens than Medium, outperforms High on SWE tasks
summary: Google released Gemini 3.5 Flash (Low), a new inference tier that generates roughly 45% fewer tokens than Flash Medium while outperforming Flash High on SWE (software engineering) benchmarks. It joins Flash Medium and High as a third option in the Flash family, providing a sharper cost-performance tradeoff for code-heavy workloads.
foryou: For agentic pipelines where output token cost is a bottleneck, Flash Low is worth benchmarking immediately. Fewer tokens plus higher SWE performance is a rare combination.

headline: Gemini 3.1 Pro: 2M-token context window, multimodal, $2/$12 per 1M tokens
summary: Gemini 3.1 Pro offers a 2 million token context window with native multimodal support across text, image, audio, and video. Pricing is $2/1M input and $12/1M output up to 200k tokens, doubling past that threshold. Cache storage runs $4.50/1M tokens/hour. Released February 2026, it represents the current context-window frontier among commercially available models.
foryou: A 2M context window means you can pass an entire codebase or a week of agent traces in a single prompt. Genuinely useful for audit, debugging, and retrospective agentic analysis.

headline: Lance: 3B-parameter multimodal model for image and video generation and editing, trained on 128 A100s
summary: Lance is a lightweight unified multimodal model supporting image and video understanding, generation, and editing. With just 3B active parameters, it delivers competitive results across image generation, editing, and video generation benchmarks. Trained entirely from scratch on a 128-A100-GPU budget, it signals strong accessibility for research teams without frontier-scale compute.
foryou: A capable image and video gen/edit model at 3B params signals that multimodal capability is rapidly democratizing. Worth tracking for lightweight agentic pipelines that need vision outputs.

headline: xAI Launches Grok Build CLI in Beta: Agentic Coding Agent with Plan Mode and Parallel Subagents
summary: Grok Build, xAI's new coding agent and CLI, has launched in beta for SuperGrok and X Premium Plus subscribers. It supports complex coding projects through a plan-review-before-action loop, headless mode for automation, and up to 8 parallel subagents. The CLI offers a 2M token context window; the API (grok-build-0.1) provides 256K tokens at $1/$2 per 1M input/output tokens.
foryou: Grok Build's headless mode and parallel subagent model makes it a direct competitor to Claude Code for agentic pipelines—worth benchmarking on repo-scale tasks, especially given the $1/1M input pricing.

headline: Google DeepMind's AlphaProof Nexus Solves 9 Open Erdős Problems for a Few Hundred Dollars Each
summary: AlphaProof Nexus, a system pairing LLMs with the Lean formal proof assistant, autonomously cracked 9 out of 353 open Erdős problems and proved 44 OEIS conjectures—including questions unanswered for decades—at an inference cost of just a few hundred dollars per problem. It uses agentic proof-checking loops: the LLM proposes a proof, Lean verifies every logical step, and the system iterates until it passes or gives up. Results were published in an arXiv preprint (2605.22763v1) on May 21, 2026.
foryou: AlphaProof Nexus is the clearest live demo of LLM+formal-verifier loops producing real scientific value—the architecture pattern (generate, formally verify, retry) is directly applicable to code correctness and specification checking in agentic pipelines.

headline: Gemini 3.5 Flash: Best at Its Speed Tier but Not Competitive with Frontier Outside Latency-Sensitive Workloads
summary: Zvi's analysis judges Gemini 3.5 Flash the best model at its speed point, outscoring Gemini 3.1 Pro on Terminal-Bench and MCP Atlas while running 4x faster. However, it is unconvincing against Opus 4.7 or GPT-5.5 for demanding tasks outside latency-sensitive agentic workflows. Google positions it as a daily driver for agents and coding.
foryou: Flash 3.5 earns its place as the cheap routing layer in multi-model pipelines—delegate fast pattern-matching and tool-dispatch steps to it, escalate complex reasoning to Opus 4.7 or GPT-5.5.

headline: BenchBench: A Benchmark That Tests Whether Models Can Design Benchmarks — Only GPT-5.2 Passed
summary: BenchBench evaluates whether a model can create a benchmark that other models find genuinely hard to solve. It tests creativity and self-awareness, not just problem-solving. In initial results, GPT-5.2 was the only model to pass; every other evaluated model—including Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.5—failed to produce a benchmark that meaningfully challenged peers.
foryou: BenchBench exposes a blind spot: strong models are not good meta-reasoners about task difficulty, which matters if you're using LLMs to auto-generate eval suites for your own pipelines.

headline: GPT-5.6 Leaked: Focused on Multi-Step Reasoning, Agentic Workflows, and Frontend Generation, Due June
summary: Leaked details suggest GPT-5.6 is heavily focused on stronger multi-step reasoning, improved agentic workflow handling, and enhanced frontend code generation capabilities. No official confirmation or pricing has been released; the model appears targeted at June 2026.
foryou: If the agentic workflow improvements are real, GPT-5.6 could shift the cost-performance calculus for orchestration-heavy pipelines—worth monitoring before committing to long-term API contracts.

headline: SpaceX rents xAI Colossus data centers to Anthropic for $15B/yr as neocloud commitments hit $130B+ in May
summary: SpaceX acquired xAI and is now renting the two Colossus data centers to Anthropic for $15 billion per year, nearly doubling SpaceX total annual revenue in a single deal. The broader AI infrastructure buildout is estimated at $7.5 trillion over 4.5 years (~5% of US GDP). May 2026 alone saw over $130B in neocloud commitments: CoreWeave backlog reached ~$100B, and Nebius secured a $27B Meta contract. Goldman Sachs projects AI infra spend will grow from $765B in 2026 to $1.6T by 2031.

headline: Anthropic on track for $10.9B Q2 revenue and $559M profit ahead of October IPO
summary: Anthropic is growing faster than Zoom at peak pandemic pace. Q2 revenue is on track for $10.9B, more than double Q1 4.8B. Claude Code alone accounts for $2.5B in revenue. Compute efficiency improved sharply: 71 cents of compute per revenue dollar in Q1 fell to 56 cents in Q2. The company expects $559M in profit and is targeting an October IPO.

headline: David Sacks kills Trump AI Executive Order on China competitiveness grounds
summary: David Sacks warned President Trump on a call that the long-awaited executive order on AI dangers risked imposing mandatory regulations that would slow the industry in its race with Chinese competitors. Trump, sharing concerns about ceding ground to China, postponed signing and told reporters he would not sign the order. The incident highlights Sacks influence over US AI policy and marks a win for those opposed to stronger guardrails.

headline: Google declares AI search the biggest change in 25 years, launches persistent information agents
summary: Google announced a complete reimagination of search powered by AI, calling it the most significant change in its history. New information agents allow users to set persistent queries that proactively surface updates, such as tracking apartment listings or market movements with specific parameters. The move signals a shift from search-as-lookup to search-as-ongoing-assistant.

headline: US government pushes mandatory pre-release AI testing; Microsoft and xAI agree to regulator early access
summary: The US government is aggressively pushing for pre-release access to frontier AI models before public deployment. Microsoft and xAI have reportedly agreed to provide regulators with early model access for testing. The move marks a significant shift in regulatory posture and may shape how model releases are globally sequenced going forward.

headline: Anthropic expands compute partnership with Google and Broadcom for multiple gigawatts of next-gen capacity
summary: Anthropic deepened its partnerships with Google and Broadcom to secure multiple gigawatts of next-generation compute capacity. The expansion comes alongside Google 40B investment in Anthropic and signals that Anthropic is locking in infrastructure for model training and inference at unprecedented scale.

headline: AI Hardware Market Is Becoming a Stack of Memory Problems, Not a Compute Race
summary: A CategoryVC analysis argues the AI hardware market has shifted: the primary bottleneck is no longer raw compute but memory bandwidth and capacity at every layer of the stack. Hardware changes slowly while software and model architectures can adapt quickly, meaning hardware vendors that don't architect for shifting memory bottlenecks will find their products obsolete ahead of schedule.

headline: Pope Leo XIV's AI Encyclical: Environmental Costs, Algorithmic Harm, and Power Concentration
summary: Pope Leo XIV released a formal document on the ethics of AI integration in modern society, touching on the technology's environmental footprint, risks of algorithmic decision-making that affects people's lives without accountability, and how AI disproportionately amplifies power for those who already have resources. The document is notable for its accessible writing style and applicability beyond Catholic audiences.

headline: DeepSeek's $10 Trillion Grand Strategy: Reshape China's AI Hardware Ecosystem, Target $1T Valuation
summary: An in-depth analysis of DeepSeek founder Liang Wenfeng's long-term ambitions reveals a goal far beyond building a model company: enable a $10 trillion Chinese AI hardware ecosystem and achieve a $1 trillion valuation within it. DeepSeek's open-source releases, aggressive price cuts, and architecture innovations are calculated to redraw global inference cost curves and position the company as the foundational layer of China's AI buildout.

headline: MCP 2026-07-28 Release Candidate: stateless protocol, OAuth-aligned, breaking changes ahead
summary: The largest MCP spec revision since launch removes the Mcp-Session-Id header and protocol-level sessions, making MCP stateless at the protocol layer. Any request can now land on any server instance with no sticky routing or shared session stores required. Apps needing state can use explicit handles passed as ordinary tool arguments. OAuth/OIDC alignment, a formal deprecation policy, and an extensions framework are also included. Final spec ships July 28; Tier 1 SDKs have a 10-week window to ship support.
foryou: If you run MCP servers in production, this is a breaking change. Plan migration now: sticky routing and shared session stores are going away at the protocol layer.

headline: OpenAI macro-eval framework: analyze populations of agent traces, not isolated failures
summary: OpenAI published a cookbook outlining a macro-evaluation workflow for agentic systems that analyzes patterns across entire populations of traces rather than debugging individual failures. The approach surfaces systemic failure modes that only emerge at scale and provides a structured methodology for validating complex multi-agent pipelines end-to-end.
foryou: Population-level trace analysis is the right framework for diagnosing systemic agent failures. Worth adapting this methodology to your own agentic pipelines before they hit production at scale.

headline: Reasonix: DeepSeek-native terminal coding agent built for prefix-cache stability and low long-session costs
summary: Reasonix is a coding agent for the terminal engineered around DeepSeek prefix-cache architecture. Designed to run persistently without breaking context, it keeps token costs low across extended sessions. Targets developers who want a cheap, always-on coding assistant embedded in their workflow without dashboard overhead.
foryou: If you want a cheap persistent coding agent in your terminal, Reasonix prefix-cache optimization means long sessions stay affordable. Worth testing against your current Claude Code workflow.

headline: Anthropic Memory Files: structured notes across topic/project/context documents coming to Claude
summary: Anthropic is planning a Claude memory update called Memory Files, which distributes stored notes across multiple structured documents organized by topic, project, or context rather than a flat memory blob. This enables more targeted retrieval and cleaner separation of concerns across long-running projects and multi-session workflows.
foryou: Memory Files could meaningfully extend Claude usefulness across multi-session agentic projects. Worth designing your workflows now to take advantage of topic-scoped memory structures when it ships.

headline: Perplexity open-sources Bumblebee: read-only security scanner targeting AI tool configs on developer machines
summary: Perplexity open-sourced Bumblebee, a read-only security scanner for developer machines that identifies risky packages, browser extensions, and AI tool configurations that may introduce security exposures. Being read-only, it provides a safe audit pass without modifying anything on the machine.
foryou: A security scanner specifically aware of AI tool configurations is a useful addition to any dev environment hardening checklist, especially as agentic tooling proliferates on developer machines.

headline: Agent-Assisted Qualitative Analysis: AI handles the mechanical parts fast, but currently lacks taste
summary: A long-form exploration of agent-assisted qualitative analysis finds that AI agents can execute the mechanical parts of reading large unstructured corpora and extracting recurring patterns at speed, but currently lack the taste to judge what is genuinely interesting or important. The post proposes experimental setups for measuring exactly where the human judgment boundary sits.
foryou: The fast-but-no-taste framing is a useful lens for deciding where to insert human review checkpoints in your own agentic pipelines that process qualitative or unstructured data.

headline: Google Antigravity 2.0: multi-agent desktop and CLI with parallel subagent spawning and cron scheduling
summary: Launched at Google I/O 2026, Antigravity 2.0 ships a desktop app, a new Go-based CLI, and an SDK for building custom agents. The core capability is dynamic subagent orchestration: the main agent spawns specialized subagents on the fly to handle focused subtasks in parallel without polluting its own context window. Cron-style scheduling lets agents run in the background automatically. The entire platform runs on Gemini 3.5 Flash, which was itself co-developed using Antigravity.
foryou: Antigravity 2.0 is a direct structural competitor to Claude Code — the parallel subagent spawning and cron scheduling mirror what you are already building with Cowork, worth benchmarking its context-isolation approach.

headline: Claude for Legal: 20+ MCP connectors and 12 practice-area plugins for end-to-end legal workflows
summary: Anthropic released a full vertical stack for legal teams: 20+ MCP connectors covering Ironclad, DocuSign, iManage, Relativity, Everlaw, and others; 12 practice-area plugins for contract review, M&A diligence, e-discovery, and regulatory monitoring; and Microsoft 365 integration that carries context across Word, Outlook, Excel, and PowerPoint. Each plugin is bootstrapped via a cold-start interview that writes a CLAUDE.md practice profile, which every subsequent skill reads from.
foryou: The CLAUDE.md practice-profile pattern — a cold-start interview that writes a persistent context file every skill reads from — is a reusable architecture pattern worth borrowing for your own multi-skill agentic pipelines.

headline: On-Policy Distillation: Close the Train-Inference Distribution Gap with One Line of Code
summary: On-policy distillation trains a student model on trajectories sampled from its own policy rather than a fixed dataset, while a teacher provides token-level KL-based supervision. This closes the train-inference distribution mismatch that off-policy methods suffer. Reverse-KL emerges as the default loss for mode-seeking smaller students, and the technique can be implemented as a one-line swap of the regularizer on top of any RL stack like Tinker.
foryou: If you're training smaller task-specific models on top of frontier outputs, on-policy distillation is the upgrade over static SFT—it makes the student behave correctly at inference time rather than just matching training distribution.

headline: Models.dev: Open API for Model Specs and Pricing Across All Major Providers
summary: Models.dev is a GitHub-hosted resource that consolidates technical specifications and pricing data for models across all major providers, accessible programmatically via an API. It serves as a single lookup layer for context windows, pricing tiers, and capability specs without manually scraping individual provider documentation.
foryou: Drop models.dev into your tooling to auto-populate model selection logic in agentic pipelines—no more hardcoding context limits or pricing per model update.
